definition: The ability to keep going through fatigue or difficulty.
example: Marathon training builds mental and physical endurance.
usageNote: Athletes train endurance with long, steady effort — not all-out sprints.

definition: How much data can travel through a network at once.
example: Video calls need more bandwidth than email.
usageNote: Figuratively: 'low bandwidth' means too many tasks, not a slow router.

definition: A huge collection of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity.
example: The Milky Way is the galaxy we live in.
usageNote: Galaxies contain billions of stars; the nearest large neighbor is Andromeda.

definition: A savory taste found in broth, mushrooms, and aged cheese.
example: Soy sauce adds umami to stir-fry.
usageNote: Umami was identified as the fifth basic taste in the early 1900s.

definition: Colors or patterns that help animals hide in plain sight.
example: The octopus changed skin texture for camouflage.
usageNote: Military uniforms use the same principle as animal camouflage.

definition: The art of persuasive speaking or writing.
example: The debate coach taught students to spot empty rhetoric.
usageNote: 'Empty rhetoric' means fine words without real substance or action.

definition: How plants convert sunlight, water, and CO₂ into sugar and oxygen.
example: Photosynthesis is why forests are called the lungs of Earth.
usageNote: Without photosynthesis, food chains would collapse — it's Earth's primary energy entry.

definition: Scrambling data so only authorized readers with a key can decode it.
example: End-to-end encryption keeps chat messages private.
usageNote: HTTPS relies on encryption between your browser and websites.

definition: A violation when an attacker is beyond the last defender too early.
example: VAR confirmed the striker was offside by a step.
usageNote: Offside rules keep attackers from camping near the goal.

definition: The variety of life forms in a habitat or on Earth.
example: Rainforests hold extraordinary biodiversity.
usageNote: Higher biodiversity usually makes ecosystems more resilient to shocks.

definition: Microbe-driven chemical change in food, such as bread rising or yogurt forming.
example: Kimchi develops its tang through fermentation.
usageNote: Fermentation preserves food and creates complex flavors.

definition: Mass times velocity; tendency to keep moving or growing.
example: The team seized momentum after a early breakaway goal.
usageNote: In sports and physics, momentum describes both literal motion and psychological streaks.
